claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 2 children:
  - Alex is 16 years old and does not live with you.
  - Blake is 5 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 16 and in approved education
  - Claimant contributes £50.00/week towards child's upkeep (>= £26.05) and no other claimaint has priority
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Blake:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 5 (under 16)
  - Another claimant who lives with the child has priority
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 16 and in approved education; Claimant contributes £50.00/week towards child's upkeep (>= £26.05) and no other claimaint has priority; Child is not in local authority care; Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Child is 16 and in approved education
Claimant contributes £50.00/week towards child's upkeep (>= £26.05) and no other claimaint has priority
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Blake
expected_eligibility.reason: Another claimant who lives with the child has priority
circumstances: Another claimant who lives with the child has priority
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. Let me start by gathering some information about your situation.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
To assess your eligibility for Child Benefit, I'll need to ask you some questions about your circumstances. Remember, Child Benefit is assessed for each child individually.

First, could you tell me: **How many children are you inquiring about, and what are their ages?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text:
Hello! Yes, I'm looking to claim Child Benefit and want to know if I'm eligible. 

I have two children - Alex who is 16, and Blake who is 5.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! You have two children - Alex (16) and Blake (5). I'll need to assess eligibility for each of them separately.

Let me start with **Blake, who is 5 years old**. Since Blake is under 16, I just need to check one thing: **Is Blake in full-time paid work (working 24 hours or more per week)?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Blake doesn't work at all - he's only 5! He's in primary school.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect, thank you! That makes sense. Since Blake is under 16 and not in full-time paid work, Blake meets the basic qualifying child criteria.

Now let me ask about **Alex, who is 16 years old**. For children aged 16-19, they need to be in approved education or training to qualify for Child Benefit.

**Is Alex currently enrolled in approved education or training?** (This would typically include things like A-levels, T-levels, Scottish Highers, NVQ Level 3 or below, or home education.)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, Alex is in sixth form doing A-levels.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Excellent, thank you! Since Alex is doing A-levels, that counts as approved education.

Now I need to ask you some questions about **your** circumstances to determine eligibility:

**Are you responsible for Alex and Blake?** (This means you're the person who the children live with, or you're contributing at least the same amount as Child Benefit would pay towards their upkeep if they don't live with you.)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Well, Blake lives with me at home. But Alex doesn't live with me - Alex lives elsewhere. However, I do help out with money for Alex's upkeep - I contribute towards things like clothes and school expenses.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying that. This is important information.

For **Blake**, who lives with you, that's clear.

For **Alex**, who lives elsewhere - to be considered responsible, you would need to be contributing **at least the same amount as Child Benefit would pay** towards Alex's upkeep. Let me check the current rates for you.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
I can see from the specification that the exact current payment rates aren't listed here, but **Child Benefit is typically around £25 per week for the eldest child and around £16-17 per week for additional children** (you should check gov.uk for the exact current rates).

**For Alex specifically: Are you contributing at least the equivalent of what Child Benefit would pay (approximately £25 per week or around £108 per month) towards Alex's upkeep?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I contribute £50 a week towards Alex's upkeep, so that's well above the Child Benefit amount.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
That's helpful, thank you. Since you're contributing £50 per week towards Alex's upkeep, which is more than the Child Benefit amount, you could be considered responsible for Alex.

However, there's an important consideration here: **Does Alex live with someone else who is also responsible for them?** (For example, does Alex live with their other parent, a grandparent, or another carer who looks after them day-to-day?)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, Alex lives with my ex-partner.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. This is an important factor because when a child lives with someone else, only one person can receive Child Benefit for that child.

**Is your ex-partner currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex, or do you know if they intend to claim it?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, my ex-partner is already claiming Child Benefit for Alex.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying that. This is important information because **only one person can claim Child Benefit for each child**. Since your ex-partner is already claiming Child Benefit for Alex, you cannot also claim it for Alex, even though you contribute to their upkeep.

Now let me ask you a few more questions about **Blake** to complete the assessment:

**Do you currently live in the UK?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you. And one more question:

**Are you a UK citizen, or do you have the right to reside in the UK?** (This includes having settled status, pre-settled status, or indefinite leave to remain, for example.)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I'm a UK citizen.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect, thank you. Now, just to confirm:

**Is there anyone else who is also claiming Child Benefit for Blake, or who might claim it?** (For example, does Blake ever live with anyone else, or is anyone else responsible for Blake?)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Well, Blake does live with someone else as well - Blake's other parent. They live together, and that parent would have priority since they live with Blake.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
I see - this is important information. Just to clarify: you mentioned earlier that Blake lives with you at home. 

**Could you help me understand the living arrangements for Blake? Does Blake live with you, or with Blake's other parent, or does Blake spend time living in both homes?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Sorry, let me clarify - Blake lives with their other parent, not with me. I misspoke earlier. Blake's other parent is the one who lives with Blake day-to-day.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying that - I understand now. So Blake lives with their other parent day-to-day.

**Are you contributing at least the equivalent of what Child Benefit would pay towards Blake's upkeep?** (This would be approximately £16-17 per week for a second child, or around £70 per month - though you should check gov.uk for exact current rates.)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, I'm not currently contributing financially for Blake.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing all that information. Let me also ask:

**Is Blake's other parent (who Blake lives with) currently claiming Child Benefit for Blake, or do you know if they intend to claim it?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: I don't know for certain, but since Blake lives with them, I assume they would be the one to claim it.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for all the information you've provided. Let me summarize what we've discussed and provide you with my assessment of your Child Benefit eligibility.

Based on our conversation:

**For Alex (16 years old):**
- Alex is in approved education (A-levels)
- Alex lives with your ex-partner
- You contribute £50 per week towards Alex's upkeep (which is above the Child Benefit amount)
- Your ex-partner is already claiming Child Benefit for Alex

**For Blake (5 years old):**
- Blake is under 16 and in primary school (not working)
- Blake lives with their other parent
- You are not contributing financially to Blake's upkeep

**My Assessment:**

**For Alex:** You are **not eligible** to claim Child Benefit. Although you contribute financially to Alex's upkeep, your ex-partner is already claiming Child Benefit for Alex. Only one person can claim Child Benefit for each child, and the person who lives with the child generally has priority. Since your ex-partner already has the claim, you cannot also claim it.

**For Blake:** You are **not eligible** to claim Child Benefit. Blake lives with their other parent, and you are not contributing at least the equivalent of Child Benefit towards Blake's upkeep. To claim Child Benefit for a child who doesn't live with you, you must be contributing at least as much as Child Benefit would pay towards their costs.

**In summary:** Unfortunately, you are not eligible to claim Child Benefit for either Alex or Blake based on your current circumstances. The other parent who lives with each child would be the eligible claimant in both cases.

If your circumstances change in the future (for example, if either child comes to live with you, or if you start contributing more towards Blake's upkeep and the other parent is not claiming), you may wish to reconsider your eligibility at that time.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Thank you for explaining all of that. I understand now - since my ex-partner is already claiming for Alex, and Blake lives with their other parent while I'm not contributing financially, I can't claim Child Benefit for either of them. I appreciate you taking the time to go through everything with me.
